summary:
• U.S.-Iran geopolitical détente regarding the Strait of Hormuz is creating near-term price pressure on crude markets, though shipping companies remain cautious about resuming full flows through this critical chokepoint.

• Alaska's oil production faces investor headwinds as domestic onshore development struggles to attract capital, potentially constraining Western Hemisphere supply growth despite untapped reserves.

• China's renewed participation in global oil markets amid inflationary pressures could intensify competition for supply from the Americas, particularly as LNG projects like Australia's Barossa come online to serve Asian demand.

articles.title: China’s Return to the Oil Market Could Boost Inflation
articles.description: The U.S.-Iran agreement to reopen the Strait of Hormuz could prompt China to return to buying more crude after months of multi-year-low purchases, which could reignite inflationary pressures despite the expected ease of oil flows from the Middle East. articles.title: Oil Prices Plunge as U.S. and Iran Reach Deal to Reopen Strait of Hormuz
articles.description: Oil prices plunged in early Monday trading in Asia after the U.S. and Iran announced a deal to reopen the Strait of Hormuz more than 100 days after its closure. At the time of writing, Brent crude had dropped 3.95% to trade at $83.88 per barrel, while WTI had fallen 4.62% to $80.
